content.text: Approval prompts

are Cowork's human-in-the-loop gate for actions that are difficult or impossible to reverse. When the engine is about to send an email, post a message to an external audience, write to a critical system, or perform any action that exceeds a workspace-configured threshold, it pauses and presents the proposed action for your review before executing.

The approval prompt shows the complete proposed action — the email body and recipient list, the Slack message and channel, the Drive document content — not a summary. You approve, reject, or modify before the engine proceeds. This gives you full visibility into what the engine is about to do in your name.

Approval prompts are not an error — they are a normal part of the goal execution flow for actions that warrant a human review step. They can be configured to appear always, only above certain thresholds (more than N email recipients, posts to public channels), or never for specific action types you trust completely.
When Approval Prompts Appear

Approval prompts are triggered by action type, scope, and workspace configuration. The default trigger set is designed to catch the actions that users most commonly regret: sending email to many recipients, posting to high-visibility channels, and writing to shared critical resources.
Default approval triggers
Action type
Default trigger
Reason
Send email
Always (first goal run per session)
Email is irreversible; a first-run approval confirms the recipient list
Email to N+ recipients
N > 5 (configurable)
Bulk sends warrant explicit review
Post to Slack (public channel)
First post per session to each channel
Posts are visible to the full workspace
Post to #general or high-follower channels
Always
High-visibility posts have broad impact
Write to a shared Drive document (not create)
Never by default
Overwriting shared docs is less common; sandbox covers this
Archive or delete a Drive file
Always
Deletion is hard to reverse
Apply Gmail labels in bulk (N+ threads)
N > 20 (configurable)
Mass label changes can be hard to undo
External API call (custom connector)
Configurable per connector
Custom connectors may call irreversible external APIs
Scheduled goals and approval prompts
Scheduled goals run without a user present. If a scheduled goal hits an action that would normally trigger an approval prompt, the behavior depends on the workspace configuration:
Default: the action is paused and a notification is sent to the goal owner; the goal waits up to 24 hours for approval
Skip-on-timeout: if no approval is received within the configured timeout, the action is skipped and logged
Auto-approve: the goal is configured to auto-approve all actions (see below) — runs fully unattended
Sandbox-and-notify: the action is run in sandbox, the report is emailed to the goal owner, and the live action waits for explicit approval
The Approval Prompt Interface

When a goal triggers an approval prompt, the Cowork interface pauses and presents a full-width panel showing the proposed action. The goal's reasoning continues to be shown in the side panel — you can see why the engine chose this action.
Prompt contents
Action type label: "Send Email", "Post to Slack", "Delete file", etc.
Full action details: for email — subject, recipient list (every address), and full email body; for Slack — channel name and complete message text
Context: the step in the goal that triggered this action and the data the engine used to produce it
Risk indicator: color-coded severity (green = low risk, yellow = medium, red = high risk such as bulk sends or external recipients)
Four action buttons: Approve, Reject, Modify, View Full Action (shows raw connector call)
Responding to a prompt
Approve

Clicking Approve executes the action immediately. The goal continues from this point. If there are subsequent approval-triggering actions in the same goal, each appears as a separate prompt in sequence.
Reject

Clicking Reject skips the action entirely. The goal receives a signal that the action was rejected and continues with its fallback behavior — if the goal specifies what to do if an action is skipped, it does that; otherwise it stops at this step and reports the rejection.

Goal text: Post the summary to #product-updates in Slack.
If the Slack post is rejected, save the summary to Drive instead.

Behavior:
- Approval prompt appears for the Slack post
- User clicks Reject
- Engine executes the fallback: saves to Drive instead (no approval needed for Drive write)
Modify

Clicking Modify opens an edit panel showing the action in editable form. For email, you can change recipients, edit the subject line, and rewrite the body. For Slack, you can change the message text or channel. After editing, you click "Approve Modified" to execute the modified version.
The modified version is logged in the goal action log with a flag indicating it was human-modified — the original proposed action is also retained for audit purposes.
Configuring Approval Prompts

Workspace admins configure the global approval policy. Individual users can adjust within the bounds the admin allows. Skill templates and plugin instructions can suggest a specific approval policy for the actions they generate.
Workspace-level configuration
In Settings → Workspace → Approval Policies, admins set:
Which action types always require approval (cannot be overridden by individual users)
Threshold values: email recipient count, Slack channel follower count above which approval is mandatory
Scheduled goal policy: pause-and-notify, skip-on-timeout, or auto-approve for specific goal categories
Retention: how long approval prompt history is retained for audit purposes
Per-goal auto-approve configuration

A goal owner can enable auto-approve for specific action types within a goal. This is appropriate for well-tested scheduled goals where the output has been validated in sandbox and the action is trusted.

Goal settings: Auto-approve configuration

Action type          Auto-approve?   Notes
Drive write          Yes             Output doc format is well-tested
Slack post           Yes             Channel #eng-weekly; team trusts content
Email send           No              Always require approval; external recipients
Delete              No              Never auto-approve delete actions

Auto-approve settings are stored per goal and per action type — they do not cascade to other goals. When a goal template is shared as a skill, auto-approve settings are not included in the skill template; each user's goal has its own approval configuration.
Bulk approval

When a goal generates multiple approval-triggering actions in one run (for example, sending individual emails to 10 account contacts), Cowork can present all of them in a batch approval view. The batch view shows all proposed actions in a list — you can approve all, reject all, or review and decide on each individually.
Batch approval is available when 3 or more actions of the same type are triggered in one goal run
Approving all in batch does not allow per-action modification — use individual review if you want to modify any action
Batch approval still logs each action individually in the audit trail
Approval Prompts in Skill Templates

A skill template can include hints about expected approval behavior, helping users know what prompts to expect when invoking the skill. This reduces surprise and helps users decide whether to test in sandbox first.
Documenting expected approvals in a skill

Skill: /investor-update
Expected approval prompts:
  - Email send (to investor list, typically 10–50 recipients) — ALWAYS prompted
  - Drive write (investor update doc) — auto-approved if Drive connector active

Note to users: test in sandbox first to review the email content and recipient
list before running in live mode. Modify the recipient list in the approval
prompt if needed — do not modify it in the goal text (the investor list is
read from Drive at runtime).

This documentation — included in the skill description or as a comment in the template — tells users what to expect before they invoke the skill, reducing the chance that an approval prompt catches them by surprise mid-goal.
Skills that generate high-risk actions

Skills that will always trigger approval prompts for irreversible, high-scope actions should document this explicitly and include a sandbox-first recommendation. A skill that sends email to external recipients or posts to a public social media account is a high-risk skill — it should always recommend a sandbox run first.
Document the maximum scope: "This skill may email up to 50 external recipients based on the CRM contact list"
Recommend sandbox: "Run in sandbox first to review the email body and recipient list before going live"
Include a modify hint: "Use the Modify option in the approval prompt to adjust the recipient list or email body if needed"
Approval Audit Trail

Every approval prompt, decision, and modification is logged in the goal's action log and retained according to the workspace's data retention policy. Workspace admins can access the approval audit trail from the Workspace → Audit Logs panel.
What the audit log records
Timestamp and goal ID for each approval prompt triggered
The complete proposed action as the engine generated it (before any modifications)
The user's decision: approved, rejected, or modified
If modified: the original proposed action AND the modified version that was executed
For auto-approved actions: logged as auto-approved with the user-set configuration noted
Using the audit trail for compliance

Organizations with communication compliance requirements (financial services, healthcare, legal) can use the approval audit trail to demonstrate that a human reviewed and approved every external communication generated by Cowork. The audit log provides:
Evidence that the communication was reviewed before sending
The original AI-proposed content (showing what the system generated)
Any human modifications made before approval
The identity of the approving user and the timestamp
Approval Prompt Patterns by Use Case
Knowing which patterns recur in practice helps you design goals and skill templates that produce approval flows your team can review confidently.
External email dispatch

Goals that send email to external recipients (outside your domain) always prompt for approval, regardless of workspace settings. This is a non-configurable safety guardrail. The prompt shows the full recipient list and email body, and allows modification before send.

Example approval prompt:

Action: Send Email
Recipients: j.smith@client.com, l.park@client.com, m.jones@client.com (3 external)
Subject: "Q3 Performance Report — Acme Corp"
Risk: MEDIUM (external recipients)

[Body preview — full email text shown here]

[ Approve ]  [ Reject ]  [ Modify email ]  [ View raw action ]
Slack broadcast to high-visibility channels

Posts to channels with large membership or channels designated as high-visibility by a workspace admin (typically #general, #announcements, #company-wide) prompt for approval on every post, not just the first. The prompt shows the channel name, member count, and full message text.
Cascading approvals in multi-step goals

A goal with several approval-triggering actions presents prompts sequentially — you approve one, the goal continues, then pauses at the next. For goals with many approvals, this can create a slow review flow. Two strategies to manage this:
Batch similar actions: structure the goal to produce all similar outputs (all email drafts, all Slack messages) at once, so they appear as a batch approval rather than sequential individual prompts
Pre-validate with sandbox: run the goal in sandbox first to see all proposed actions in one view; once satisfied, run live knowing you will approve each prompt as expected
Common Approval Prompt Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
The most common mistakes with approval prompts involve either bypassing them inappropriately or designing goals that generate so many prompts they become burdensome.
Over-trusting auto-approve

Setting all actions to auto-approve defeats the purpose of the prompt system. Auto-approve is appropriate for well-tested actions in stable environments — Drive writes to a folder where the format and destination are predictable. It is not appropriate for external email sends or posts to public channels, regardless of how many sandbox runs the goal has passed.

A rule of thumb: never auto-approve actions that have an external audience. Internal-only actions (writing to your Drive, posting to a private team channel) are good candidates for auto-approve after thorough sandbox validation. External-facing actions (email, public posts, external API calls) should always require a human approval step.
Goals that generate excessive prompts
A goal that sends an individual email to each of 50 contacts will generate 50 sequential approval prompts — an unreasonable review burden. Restructure such goals:
Batch into one send with all recipients in CC or BCC (one prompt for the full send)
Use a distribution list that the user controls (one prompt, the list handles individual delivery)
Send one summary email to the requestor with the individual emails as attachments (one prompt, one send)
If individual sends are required, use the batch approval view and review a sample of the emails rather than approving each individually
Modifying at the prompt rather than fixing the goal

If you consistently modify the same field in an approval prompt (changing the recipient, fixing a subject line, removing a CC), the fix belongs in the goal text or skill template — not in the approval prompt. Prompts are for edge-case corrections and final review, not for routine adjustments that should be automated.
If you always change the recipient list: add a {{recipient_list}} placeholder to the skill template
If you always remove a CC address: specify the recipients explicitly in the goal text instead of using a connector-derived list
If you always fix the subject line: add a {{subject}} placeholder or hardcode the format in the template
Approval Prompts and Goal Reliability

Approval prompts affect scheduled goal reliability because they require a human to be present and available. A scheduled goal that generates approval prompts will delay its outputs until a human responds — this is by design, but it means you need to account for review latency in time-sensitive workflows.
Strategies for time-sensitive scheduled goals
Strategy
How it works
Best for
Auto-approve trusted actions
Set specific action types to auto-approve for the goal
Internal-only writes with predictable format and destination
Sandbox-and-notify
Run in sandbox, email report to owner, wait for explicit approval before live run
Compliance workflows where a record of review is required
Dual-goal structure
First goal assembles and writes to Drive (auto-approved); second goal (triggered manually) reads from Drive and sends externally (prompted)
Automated assembly + human-gated distribution
Pre-approved templates
Use templated output that was approved once; subsequent runs of the same template auto-approve
Recurring reports where content changes but structure is fixed
Summary

Approval prompts pause goal execution before irreversible actions and present the complete proposed action for human review. They are the primary mechanism for maintaining oversight over Cowork's write and send operations.
Triggered by action type and scope: email sends, Slack posts to public channels, bulk label operations, and file deletions trigger by default
Three responses: Approve (execute as-is), Reject (skip and use fallback), Modify (edit then execute)
Scheduled goals: paused and queued for approval or configured to auto-approve for trusted action types
Workspace admins set the global policy; goal owners can configure per-goal auto-approve within admin bounds
Skills should document expected approval prompts and recommend sandbox testing for high-risk actions
Full audit trail retained for every prompt, decision, and modification
